title: Theory of supercooled water
abstract: We introduce a model of water contemplating true supercooled-liquid states that, as such, are metastable with respect to the crystalline-solid ones. Its numerical solutions reproduce from Speedy-Angell's stability-limit picture to Poole et al.'s metastable liquid--liquid criticality. The real existence of a liquid--liquid critical point is found to gain significant theoretical plausibility from a hitherto unnoticed analogy with the ``inversion thermodynamics'' of Joule-Thomson gas throttling process and, by extension, with the equivalent phenomenology exhibited by black holes.
summary: This paper presents a versatile five-state Ising-like model that can theoretically account for both the stability-limit and liquid-liquid criticality theories of supercooled water. It introduces a novel connection between the thermodynamic anomalies of water and the inversion thermodynamics found in both classical gases and black hole physics.
main_problem: Resolving the debate between the stability-limit and liquid-liquid criticality scenarios in supercooled water by providing a unified theoretical framework.
main_result: The authors develop a model that can reproduce both competing scenarios and propose a new thermodynamic link between water's anomalies and the inversion thermodynamics seen in Joule-Thomson gases and black holes.
method: A mean-field treatment of a five-state Ising-like model using a generalization of single-order-parameter theory with four order parameters.
model_or_system: A five-state Ising-like model where lattice sites are 'cells' defined by occupancy, volume, free volume, and particle orientations, representing gas, liquid (HDL/LDL), and crystal (HDC/LDC) phases.
key_observables: Isothermal compressibility (kappa_T), isobaric heat capacity (cp), thermal expansivity (alpha_p), density (rho), pressure (p), and temperature (T).
important_parameters: Temperature of Maximum Density (TMD) locus, liquid-liquid critical point (LLCP), and the Joule-Thomson inversion locus.
main_assumptions: The use of mean-field theory in three dimensions and the treatment of the crystal phase as a spectator phase rather than modeling specific ice lattice structures.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the five cell states; Figure 2 displays the stability-limit scenario with p-rho isotherms and p-T phase diagrams; Figure 3 shows the liquid-liquid criticality scenario; Figure 4 compares water simulation results with argon experimental data and provides a table of thermodynamic analogies.
paper_structure: Introduction of the problem, description of the five-state cell model, presentation of the stability-limit scenario, presentation of the liquid-liquid criticality scenario, discussion of the thermodynamic inversion analogy with gases and black holes, and concluding remarks.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Two-dimensional illustration of individual cell states of our five-state Ising model. Shaded in grey are the free volumes explored by particles (crosses). Associated with each cell state is a phase: see text.

FIG. 2: Numerical results for our five-state model with parameter setting “1.” (Left) Pressure p versus density ρ along an isotherm of temperature T. Three independent branches display stable and metastable states for fluid, LDC, and HDC phases, dashed lines being unstable sections. The inset shows the fluid branch at a lower T. (Right) Corresponding phase diagram in the pressure-temperature p-T plane. Plotted are binodals (thick, grey), spinodals (thin, orange), and the locus of temperatures of maximum density along isobars TMD (thin, blue). Special points are critical c, triple t, and s points. Continuations of crystal spinodals beyond their crossing point are not drawn for clarity of...

Figure 1 illustrates the molecular details featured by our five cell states. We distinguish between vacant cells and cells occupied by a nonspherical particle with ori- entational degrees of freedom whose center of mass ex- plores a free volume. A given state is then defined by cell’s occupancy, volume, free volume, and number of particle’s orientations, all of which regarded in general distinct for distinct states [47]. The fluctuating nature of cell volumes and number of particle’s orientations al- lows to characterize phases with distinct densities and hydrogen-bond network efficiencies [29, 31, 34]. In turn, fluctuating free volumes distinguish the positional or- der of crystalline...

FIG. 3: Numerical results for our five-state model with pa- rameter setting “2.” Plotted in the p-T plane are binodals (thick, grey), spinodals (thin, orange), and the locus of tem- peratures of maximum density along isobars TMD (thin, blue). Special points are critical c, triple t, and s points. Con- tinuations of crystal spinodals beyond their crossing point are not drawn for clarity of presentation; likewise for the freezing binodals.

FIG. 4: (Left) Molecular simulation results for the TIP4P/2005 water force field [13, 53–55]. (Right) Experimen- tal data for argon [56]. The upper panels display isobars in the temperature-volume per particle T-v plane and isotherms in the pressure-enthalpy per particle p-h plane, with binodal curves in grey. The lower panels show the corresponding TMD and Joule-Thomson JT inversion loci (blue) together with the loci of isobaric extrema of the isothermal compressibility κT and isothermal extrema of the isobaric heat capacity per particle cp (brown) respectively emanating from the water’s liquid–liquid critical point LLCP and argon’s gas–liquid crit- ical point GLCP.
